mechanism_narrative: Real retail sales (RSAFS) exhibit a persistent upward trend driven by consumer spending and income growth. The continuation of this trend signals economic expansion, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days as corporate earnings benefit from demand.
evidence_refs: fact:Retail sales have been steadily increasing over the past year.
failure_condition: If RSAFS declines over any 3-month period and S&P 500 does not decline in the following 20 trading days in at least 70% of such instances, the hypothesis fails. Also, if the rank IC of RSAFS growth rate with forward 20-day returns is less than 0.05 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate rises above a threshold of 4.5%, it signals a weakening labor market that precedes consumer spending declines and earnings downgrades. This leads to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a 60-trading-day horizon as the economy decelerates.
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE snapshot is 4.6%, already above threshold as of 2007-08-15.
failure_condition: If the average forward 60-day return when UNRATE > 4.5% is not negative and significantly different from periods when UNRATE <= 4.5% (t-test p < 0.05), the hypothesis is rejected. Also if the signal fails to predict negative returns in more than 50% of triggered instances.

mechanism_narrative: High federal funds rate combined with declining housing starts indicates that restrictive monetary policy is successfully cooling the overheating housing sector, but at the cost of broader economic slowdown. This joint signal predicts negative S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days as recession fears mount.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS > 5.0% and HOUST declining by more than 5% year-over-year
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST declined from 1493 in June 2006 to 1467 in June 2007 (year-over-year decline).
failure_condition: If the interaction term (FEDFUNDS * HOUST change) has a rank IC below 0.03 with forward 20-day returns over any 2-year window, or if the signal fails to produce negative mean returns when both conditions are met (t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: In a regime of negative GDP growth (recession), a rising unemployment rate amplifies the negative impact on consumer spending and corporate earnings, leading to strongly negative S&P 500 returns over a 60-trading-day horizon. The signal is only active when the economy is in recession.
evidence_refs: fact:GDP growth may be slowing; GDPC1 snapshot is 11507.9 Q2 2007, Q1 was 11487.9, so positive but could be near turning point.
failure_condition: If during periods of negative GDP growth (GDPC1 < previous year), rising UNRATE does not predict negative forward 60-day returns with correlation < -0.2, the hypothesis is rejected. Also if the signal only activates in less than 5% of the sample, it may be too rare.

mechanism_narrative: A decline in industrial production below a level of 110 signals a contraction in manufacturing activity, which historically precedes recessions and corporate earnings collapses. This threshold crossing predicts negative S&P 500 returns over a 20-trading-day horizon as investors anticipate weaker profits.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO peaked around 114 in early 2007 and is now 113.86, approaching the threshold.
failure_condition: If the forward 20-day return after INDPRO crosses below 110 is not negative on average (mean negative with p < 0.10 one-sided), or if the threshold yields fewer than 10 trigger events in the walk-forward period, the hypothesis fails.

mechanism_narrative: Rising consumer price inflation coupled with low unemployment creates a Phillips curve tension that forces the Federal Reserve to tighten monetary policy, which subsequently depresses equity valuations. The joint observation of high CPI and low unemployment signals future tightening and negative S&P 500 returns over a 20-trading-day horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:CPI is 208.028 as of June 2007, well above 205. Unemployment is 4.6%, below 5%.
regime_hint: tight labor market with inflation pressure
failure_condition: If the interaction between CPI level and unemployment inverse does not produce a rank IC < -0.05 with forward 20-day returns, or if the mean return during joint condition is not negative, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: In a regime of high federal funds rate (above 5%), the Fed's commitment to fighting inflation intensifies the negative effect of rising consumer prices on equity markets. When the Fed is already tight, further inflation leads to even more restrictive actions, causing negative S&P 500 returns over a 5-trading-day horizon as markets react quickly.
failure_condition: If in periods where FEDFUNDS > 5%, the correlation between CPI change and forward 5-day returns is not significantly negative (Pearson r < -0.1, p < 0.05), or if the signal fails to generate negative mean returns.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts falling below 1.4 million units signals a severe contraction in the housing market, which historically leads to broader economic recessions and steep equity declines. This threshold-triggered signal predicts negative S&P 500 returns over a 60-trading-day horizon as the housing slump deepens.
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST is 1467 as of June 2007, down from 1493 a year ago but still above 1400.
failure_condition: If the average forward 60-day return after HOUST falls below 1400 is not negative (mean negative with p < 0.10), or if the signal occurs fewer than 5 times in the history, the hypothesis is rejected.
